title: Evaluation Portfolio Prioritization Framework
preview:
Purpose:
This document defines a lightweight, repeatable framework for maintaining and prioritizing Redwood's evaluation portfolio across eval-harness runs, prompt-benchmarks, onboarding checks, quantization profiles, and regression triage exercises. The goal is to ensure eval work is aligned to product impact, risk...
text:
Purpose:
This document defines a lightweight, repeatable framework for maintaining and prioritizing Redwood's evaluation portfolio across eval-harness runs, prompt-benchmarks, onboarding checks, quantization profiles, and regression triage exercises. The goal is to ensure eval work is aligned to product impact, risk, and operational cost so we run the right tests at the right cadence and react quickly to detected regressions.

Scope:
- Applies to all evaluation activities owned by Applied ML & Evals, Model Onboarding, and Runtime teams.
- Covers both offline (batch) evals and lightweight online/serving simulations.
- Does not replace specific test implementations or labeling processes; it provides a prioritization and scheduling layer.

Key definitions:
- Evaluation asset: any runnable test, prompt set, or benchmark (examples: long-context-robustness suite, prompt-coverage matrix, qua...

title: Instrumentation Sunset and Cleanup Playbook
preview:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes the approved, low-risk process to retire (sunset) unused or harmful instrumentation signals, remove stale dashboards/alerts, and reclaim telemetry budget. It's intended for platform engineers, telemetry owners, and oncall operators. Follow the steps below to minimize alert c...
text:
Overview
--------
This playbook describes the approved, low-risk process to retire (sunset) unused or harmful instrumentation signals, remove stale dashboards/alerts, and reclaim telemetry budget. It's intended for platform engineers, telemetry owners, and oncall operators. Follow the steps below to minimize alert churn, SLO regressions, and data-loss surprises.

When to use this playbook
-------------------------
- A metric or log signal has no active consumer for >= 90 days.
- An alert fires repeatedly but has no owner or actionability (signal noise).
- A dashboard is irrelevant after a major architecture change (service split, deprecation).
- Telemetry cost budget is exhausted by high-cardinality data that provides low value.

Goals and constraints
---------------------
- Ensure no customer-facing SLOs are degraded by telemetry removal.
- Maintain auditability: every sunset must ha...

title: Evaluation Asset Registry and Evaluator Service Contract
preview:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The E...
text:
Summary:
This document defines the Evaluation Asset Registry (EAR) — a canonical catalog and lifecycle for evaluation artifacts — and the Evaluator Service Contract (ESC) that runtime and eval components must implement to consume, produce, and promote assets safely across shadow, canary, and production stages. The EAR + ESC are intended to reduce duplicated probe creation, improve reproducibility, and provide clear operational guarantees for model onboarding, quantization, and regression triage workflows.

Goals and non-goals:
- Goals:
 - Provide a single, queryable registry of evaluation assets (probe sets, slice tags, sample annotations, golden outputs, evaluator configs, telemetry fingerprints, quant-compatibility reports).
 - Standardize metadata such that evaluators, CI, and the serving runtime can make deterministic promotion/rollback decisions.
 - Define SLOs and behavioral exp...

title: Streaming Error Taxonomy and Retry Contract
preview:
Overview:
This page defines a company-wide error taxonomy and client-facing retry contract specifically for streaming endpoints (server-sent events, HTTP/2/gRPC streaming, and chunked streaming). It supplements existing API compatibility and typed-error guidelines by focusing on streaming-specific failure modes, res...
text:
Overview:
This page defines a company-wide error taxonomy and client-facing retry contract specifically for streaming endpoints (server-sent events, HTTP/2/gRPC streaming, and chunked streaming). It supplements existing API compatibility and typed-error guidelines by focusing on streaming-specific failure modes, resume semantics, and SLO-safe retry behavior. The goal is to make client behaviour predictable, reduce unnecessary load from aggressive retries, and provide observability hooks that map streaming errors to operational responses and SLIs.

Audience:
- API owners and backend engineers implementing streaming endpoints
- Client SDK authors and integrators
- SRE and monitoring owners responsible for SLIs/alerting

Scope and applicability:
- Applies to all external-facing streaming endpoints and internal long-lived RPC streams used for inference (chat, generation streams, long-poll...

Purpose
-------
This page defines Redwood Inference's standardized procurement approval matrix, the lifecycle steps for contract execution, and the precise billing triggers that feed the revenue recognition (revrec) process. It is intended for Finance, Legal, Procurement owners, and Engineering or Product teams who initiate supplier commitments. The goal is to reduce cycle time, ensure correct accounting treatment at go-live, and avoid revenue/billing misalignment across hosted, dedicated, and private product lines.

Scope
-----
Applies to: all third-party software and hardware purchases, cloud/hosted commitments, professional services, and vendor contracts that create a legal obligation or financial commitment for Redwood Inference (including pilot agreements and proof-of-concept statements of work).
Excludes: petty cash, employee expense reimbursements, and subscriptions procured on...

title: Econometric Latency-Cost Sensitivity Model and Simulation Runbook
preview:
Summary:
This document defines an econometric model for estimating how changes in runtime decisions (kernel selection, batching, quantization, routing) map to observed latency and per-token cost, and provides a reproducible simulation runbook for validating sensitivity hypotheses before production rollouts. The goal...
text:
Summary:
This document defines an econometric model for estimating how changes in runtime decisions (kernel selection, batching, quantization, routing) map to observed latency and per-token cost, and provides a reproducible simulation runbook for validating sensitivity hypotheses before production rollouts. The goal is to provide a single reference for analysts and runtime engineers to: quantify marginal cost/latency effects, prioritize optimization work, and validate safe rollout envelopes for changes that affect operator economics or SLOs.

Contents:
1) Introduction and intent
2) Model specification and assumptions
3) Required telemetry and inputs
4) Simulation lab setup and dataset construction
5) Runbook: experiment steps (numbered)
6) Analysis and decision criteria
7) Example configs and sample output
8) Ownership and escalation
9) Appendix: quick CLI snippets and sanity checks

title: Fleet Canary Allocator Terraform Module and Runbook
preview:
Summary

This document defines the design, Terraform module interface, CI/CD integration, and on-call runbook for the Fleet Canary Allocator: a small control-plane component that performs safe, SLO-driven gradual rollouts of allocation rules and GPU placement policies across heterogeneous GPU pools. The primary goal...
text:
Summary

This document defines the design, Terraform module interface, CI/CD integration, and on-call runbook for the Fleet Canary Allocator: a small control-plane component that performs safe, SLO-driven gradual rollouts of allocation rules and GPU placement policies across heterogeneous GPU pools. The primary goal is to minimize blast radius for policy changes that affect tenant placement, preemption behavior, and burst capacity while keeping rollout automation auditable and reversible.

Why this exists

- We frequently change allocation policies (fair-share weights, priority buckets, spot fallback policy) and kernel/runtime flags that affect performance and preemption probability.
- A full fleet-wide change can cause regressions in latency/SLOs or tenant evictions if there is an unseen interaction with topology or the scheduler.
- Existing canary tooling targets provisioning; this ...

title: ADR-0231: Require quality evaluation gates for Optimize config changes
preview:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective for Hosted API Optimize applies starting limited production rollout).

## Decision
Redwood Inference will require **quality evaluation gates** to pass **before** any Optimize recommendation (or manual Optimize-driven change) can be applied to **production traffic** for the following...
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (effective for Hosted API Optimize applies starting limited production rollout).

## Decision
Redwood Inference will require **quality evaluation gates** to pass **before** any Optimize recommendation (or manual Optimize-driven change) can be applied to **production traffic** for the following config change types:

- **Batching policy updates** (e.g., max batch size, continuous batching parameters)
- **Prefix/KV caching settings** (e.g., cache enablement, eviction policies, cache keying changes)
- **Quantization profile changes** (e.g., fp16 -> int8, model-family-specific quant profiles per GPU SKU)

Gate enforcement is **mandatory** for:
- One-click “Apply” in Console
- Any Optimize auto-apply mechanism
- Canary promotion in rollout controller (defense-in-depth)

A change may only proceed when:
1) **Static validation** passes (unsupported combinations rejected ...

title: ADR-023: Workload-aware batching defaults via three SLO tiers (fast / standard / cost-efficient)
preview:
## Status
**Accepted** (published)

## Decision
We will ship **three customer-facing batching default profiles** aligned to **latency SLO tiers**:
- **fast** (tail-latency protection)
- **standard** (balanced default)
- **cost-efficient** (throughput/$ optimization)

Defaults will be **workload-aware within the tier...
text:
## Status
**Accepted** (published)

## Decision
We will ship **three customer-facing batching default profiles** aligned to **latency SLO tiers**:
- **fast** (tail-latency protection)
- **standard** (balanced default)
- **cost-efficient** (throughput/$ optimization)

Defaults will be **workload-aware within the tier** (using a small set of runtime signals such as prompt-length buckets, streaming vs non-streaming, and observed concurrency/queue pressure), but we will **not** ship **per-tenant auto-tuned batching** in the initial release.

We will provide a clear **override hierarchy** and **reason codes** so operators can understand and control effective behavior:
1) Customer override (Dedicated/Enterprise) 
2) Route/model policy selection (control plane)
3) Tier default (fast/standard/cost-efficient)
4) Safety fallback / overload mode constraints (runtime)

## Context
Redwood’s infere...

text: A change to the runtime memory watermark defaults caused aggressive KV-cache eviction under bursty workloads, leading to repeated recomputation and elevated latency.

text:
Overview

This playbook documents the integrated authorization (authZ), audit-evidence, data residency, secure-SDLC signoffs, and incident-response steps for executing blue/green deployments of model-serving components or inference pipelines. It is intended for platform engineers, release owners, security, and compliance reviewers. The goal is to ensure every blue/green switch is auditable end-to-end, minimizes risk to data locality and entitlement drift, and provides forensic-ready artifacts for regulators and customers.

Scope and applicability

- Applies to: model-serving clusters, inference feature flags, model artifacts, and associated routing components across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments.
- Excludes: data scientist experiments in notebooks (handled by the sandbox governance doc).
- Trigger conditions: any production change that results in a live model change, rout...

title: Proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging runbook
preview:
Summary:

This runbook describes the proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging process used by Redwood Inference SRE to detect early signals of service instability, isolate affected traffic (quarantine), and allocate short-term capacity hedges to preserve SLOs while a remediation is performed. It ties direct...
text:
Summary:

This runbook describes the proactive anomaly quarantine and capacity hedging process used by Redwood Inference SRE to detect early signals of service instability, isolate affected traffic (quarantine), and allocate short-term capacity hedges to preserve SLOs while a remediation is performed. It ties directly to SLO thresholds and error budget policies and is intended for oncall, incident commanders, and automation owners.

Scope:
- Applies to customer-facing inference endpoints (hosted API) and inter-service routing between control-plane components and serving runtime.
- Not a replacement for full incident response; this is a containment and mitigation layer used before escalation to a SEV.

Why this exists:
- Transient anomalies (kernel/backpressure, noisy tenants, model-level regressions) can erode SLOs quickly. Rapid isolation + capacity hedging preserves customer experie...

title: Authorization Snapshotting and Retention Playbook

text:
Overview

This playbook defines a cross-functional standard for creating, storing, and packaging point-in-time snapshots of authorization state and related telemetry across Redwood Inference services. It covers: RBAC and auth configuration capture, associated audit log evidence, retention schedules with data residency considerations, SDLC gates to ensure evidenceable changes, incident response usage, and exception handling. The goal is reproducible artifacts for audits and investigations while minimizing surface area for sensitive data exposure.

Scope and Intent

- Systems: identity provider (IdP), internal entitlement service (entd), gateway authz policies, tenant metadata store, CI/CD change history, and audit log pipelines (Splunk/BigQuery).
- Use cases: compliance audits, breach investigation, post-incident root cause, quarterly evidence packaging for regulated customers.

title: Cost-aware streaming payload schema and metadata guideline
preview:
Overview:

This guideline defines a standardized payload schema and runtime metadata conventions for cost-aware streaming inference requests across Redwood’s public and private deployments. It is intended for API engineers, SDK maintainers, runtime routing components, and observability pipelines. The goal is to make...
text:
Overview:

This guideline defines a standardized payload schema and runtime metadata conventions for cost-aware streaming inference requests across Redwood’s public and private deployments. It is intended for API engineers, SDK maintainers, runtime routing components, and observability pipelines. The goal is to make per-request cost signals explicit, machine-readable, and compatible with our streaming and batch transports.

Why this matters:
- Enables accurate per-request cost attribution and billing for streamed tokens and partial outputs.
- Allows runtime routing decisions (e.g., route to cheaper quantized variant) based on declared request constraints.
- Improves observability: deterministic dimensions for SLOs, per-token latencies, and partial-output error attribution.

Scope:
- Applies to: text generation streaming endpoints (server-sent events, chunked HTTP, gRPC streams) and ch...

title: Occupancy and Concurrency Audit Playbook for Serving Runtime
preview:
Overview:

This playbook describes a repeatable audit process to assess and remediate GPU occupancy and request concurrency issues in the Redwood serving runtime. It is intended for on-call engineers, performance engineers, and SREs responsible for multi-tenant inference pools and kernel scheduler tuning. The goal i...
text:
Overview:

This playbook describes a repeatable audit process to assess and remediate GPU occupancy and request concurrency issues in the Redwood serving runtime. It is intended for on-call engineers, performance engineers, and SREs responsible for multi-tenant inference pools and kernel scheduler tuning. The goal is to detect pathological occupancy/concurrency patterns early, quantify their impact on latency/cost, and prescribe targeted mitigations.

Why this matters:

- Low GPU occupancy (SM utilization) or poor warp efficiency increases per-token cost and can drastically raise tail latency for short requests.
- Excessive concurrency without appropriate kernel crediting can cause kernel contention, eviction churn, and KV-cache thrash.
- Multi-tenant pools and dynamic batching increase the surface area for occupancy anomalies; automated audits help prevent regressions when we change ...

Goals

title: Incident Seeding and Warm Recovery Playbook
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting cust...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This playbook explains our controlled incident seeding practice and a standard warm-recovery runloop for LLM-serving incidents. Incident seeding is a deliberate, low-risk exercise where we inject small, observable faults to validate detection, routing, and warm-handoff paths without impacting customer-facing SLOs. Warm recovery is a set of prioritized, pre-authorized mitigations that restore service quality while preserving user sessions and KV cache state.

Scope
- Services: routing-proxy, runtime-gateway, kv-cache, model-pool, rate-limiter.
- Regions: us-west-2, eu-central-1.
- Tenancy: multitenant hosted API and Dedicated pools.

Definitions
- Seeding event: a controlled fault or degradation introduced for testing (e.g., 20% KV cache eviction, isolated model fallback).
- Warm handoff: transferring active incident ownership from automated systems to oncall with mi...

title: GPU build fan-out and stampede mitigation guide
preview:
Overview

Purpose: This guide describes patterns, Terraform/IaC examples, monitoring signals, and an operational runbook to prevent and mitigate GPU stampedes and uncontrolled fan-out from CI pipelines and evaluation jobs. It targets infra engineers, CI owners, and platform SREs who operate GPU pools used for builds...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This guide describes patterns, Terraform/IaC examples, monitoring signals, and an operational runbook to prevent and mitigate GPU stampedes and uncontrolled fan-out from CI pipelines and evaluation jobs. It targets infra engineers, CI owners, and platform SREs who operate GPU pools used for builds, evaluations, and canary workloads.

Summary: When large CI/eval jobs scale concurrently (nightly evals, multi-PR dependency checks, or mass rollouts), we observe sudden demand spikes that cause long queue times, warm-pool depletion, preemptions, and downstream latency for customer-facing inference. The mitigation strategy here is multi-layered: admission control + backpressure at CI, rate-limited fan-out adapters, autoscaler safeguards, pre-warm and warm-pool budgeting, token-bucket throttles, and telemetry-driven emergency throttles.

Key goals
- Ensure 95th percentile C...

Summary

This document is a laboratory-grade procedure set for deterministic fault injection against the runtime scheduler and kernel handoff surface. - Verify observability and automated recovery paths capture and mitigate incidents.

Scope

title: Customer-managed audit log export: security controls and approval gates
preview:
# Purpose
This page defines the **mandatory security controls** and **approval gates** for enabling **customer-managed audit log export** from Redwood Inference to customer-controlled cloud storage (e.g., S3/GCS/Azure Blob) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

This is a control surface that touches: c...
text:
# Purpose
This page defines the **mandatory security controls** and **approval gates** for enabling **customer-managed audit log export** from Redwood Inference to customer-controlled cloud storage (e.g., S3/GCS/Azure Blob) across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

This is a control surface that touches: customer data boundaries, identity and access, encryption, and retention. **Do not enable export** without satisfying the requirements below.

# Scope
In scope:
- Export of **platform audit logs** (auth events, RBAC changes, API key lifecycle, console/admin actions, private control-plane actions).
- Delivery to a **customer-owned destination**.

Out of scope:
- Model inference payload logging (prompt/completion content). That remains disabled by default and requires a separate data logging exception.

# Definitions
- **Audit log**: event records emitted by Redwood control pl...

title: Inference Request Log Retention Policy
preview:
# Purpose
This policy defines Redwood Inference’s default retention for *inference request logs* and the process for customer-specific exceptions.

# Scope
Applies to Hosted API and Dedicated deployments operated by Redwood. For Private deployments, customer-managed logging may apply; however, any Redwood-operated t...
text:
# Purpose
This policy defines Redwood Inference’s default retention for *inference request logs* and the process for customer-specific exceptions.

# Scope
Applies to Hosted API and Dedicated deployments operated by Redwood. For Private deployments, customer-managed logging may apply; however, any Redwood-operated telemetry collection still follows this policy unless explicitly excepted.

# Definitions
**Inference request logs** ("request logs") include: request/response bodies (prompt + completion), request metadata (model, tokens, latency, status code), and routing identifiers.

# Default retention
Unless otherwise approved as an exception:
- **Request/response bodies (prompt + completion): 30 days**
- **Request metadata (no prompt/completion text): 90 days**
- **Aggregated metrics (no per-request identifiers): 13 months**

# Storage locations (summary)
- Primary log store: `obs-log...

text:
Overview

Purpose: This document defines recommended SDK design patterns for tenant-scoped authentication and robust local fallbacks. Its goal is to reduce support incidents and production outages in multi-tenant applications that call Redwood inference endpoints. The guidance is written for SDK implementers (client libraries and server-side helper libs) and for developer-facing code patterns used by product teams.

Intended audience:
- SDK engineers implementing Redwood client libraries
- Platform engineers integrating tenant-aware routing
- Support engineers troubleshooting tenant-impacted incidents

Why this matters
- Multi-tenant workloads commonly fail in two categories: auth/identity mispropagation and global rate-limit storms.
- Proper tenant-scoped tokens, local caches, and graceful fallbacks prevent noisy neighbor effects and provide predictable user experience when upstream ...

title: API deterministic output samples and canonical fixes

It is intended for SDK authors, integration engineers, and support engineers who triage output variance regressions or...
text:
Overview

This guide documents patterns, quickstart snippets, telemetry hooks, and canonical fixes for achieving reproducible (deterministic) API outputs when using Redwood SDKs and the Redwood API. It is intended for SDK authors, integration engineers, and support engineers who triage output variance regressions or write tests/benchmarks that require repeatable responses.

Why deterministic outputs matter

- Evals and regression tests depend on repeatable responses when measuring model drift or SDK changes.
- Customer integrations (prompt templates, scoring) sometimes rely on stable tokenization and sampling outputs for business logic.
- Determinism reduces flakiness in CI and helps isolate runtime vs model vs prompt issues.

Scope and assumptions

- Applies to text generation APIs (chat/generate/stream) and to SDKs wrapping those endpoints.
- Does not attempt to override model-level...

title: Customer‑sensitive Notice Lifecycle for Degraded Inference
preview:
Summary
This playbook defines the lifecycle and templates for communicating with high‑sensitivity customers (enterprise, regulated) during inference quality or availability degradations. It standardizes timings, ownership, escalation paths, and message content so notices are predictable, legally defensible, and alig...
text:
Summary
This playbook defines the lifecycle and templates for communicating with high‑sensitivity customers (enterprise, regulated) during inference quality or availability degradations. It standardizes timings, ownership, escalation paths, and message content so notices are predictable, legally defensible, and aligned with SLAs/SLOs.

Scope and definitions
- Applies to incidents affecting: text generation API, chat, embeddings, reranking, or routing logic that can materially affect customer output quality or availability.
- High‑sensitivity customer: an account with contractual notice SLAs, data residency constraints, or regulated workloads.
- Severity tiers used here map to company SLOs:
 - Severity 1 (S1): Total service outage or >50% customer impact on request success for >5 minutes.
 - Severity 2 (S2): Significant degradation (latency >3x P95 or quality regressions for critical r...

text:
h1. Purpose
This standard defines what must be true for a model version to be considered production-ready on Redwood Inference. Rollout plans MUST reference the readiness artifacts listed here.

h1. Definitions
* Model version: immutable build of weights + tokenizer + runtime settings, identified by a version id.
* Compatibility: whether a version can serve a given request type (chat/completions/embeddings/rerank) and feature set (streaming, tool calling, structured output).

h1. Required readiness artifacts (all production versions)
A model version is READY_FOR_PROD only when the following are complete and linked in the model registry entry:

h2. A) Functional compatibility
* Supported request types: chat, completions, embeddings, rerank (list explicitly)
* Streaming support: yes/no and known limitations
* Tool/function calling: supported schemas and known differences
* Structured ou...

title: Terraform policy + network canary: GPU egress validation harness
preview:
Summary:
This document describes a repeatable, policy-driven validation harness we use during GPU tenant onboarding to ensure Terraform modules, VPC/subnet/network ACLs, and egress controls meet Redwood Inference networking and security requirements before production promotion. The harness combines: Terraform plan v...
text:
Summary:
This document describes a repeatable, policy-driven validation harness we use during GPU tenant onboarding to ensure Terraform modules, VPC/subnet/network ACLs, and egress controls meet Redwood Inference networking and security requirements before production promotion. The harness combines: Terraform plan validation, OPA/Rego policy checks, a CI/CD canary stage that deploys lightweight network probes, and synthetic inference traffic checks to validate egress, routes, and KV cache behavior under bounded load.

Goals:
- Prevent misconfigured network paths that would allow uncontrolled egress or violate tenant isolation SLOs.
- Automate policy-as-code checks early in CI to fail unsafe changes pre-merge.
- Provide a canary runtime test that validates real network behavior and telemetry before full rollout.
- Provide clear rollback/mitigation actions and owner escalation for failu...

title: Multi-signal Priority Lattice and Decision Runbook
preview:
Summary:

This runbook defines a multi-signal prioritization lattice used by platform operators when multiple alerts and business signals fire simultaneously. The lattice converts heterogeneous signals (latency, token-errors, quality regressions, customer impact, payment deltas, and telemetry noise) into a ranked ac...
text:
Summary:

This runbook defines a multi-signal prioritization lattice used by platform operators when multiple alerts and business signals fire simultaneously. The lattice converts heterogeneous signals (latency, token-errors, quality regressions, customer impact, payment deltas, and telemetry noise) into a ranked action queue with clear ownership and operator instructions. The goal is to: reduce time-to-mitigation for high-impact events, limit unnecessary escalations, and preserve customer SLAs.

When to use:
- Multi-region or multi-model incidents where more than two distinct alert streams are active concurrently.
- When the root cause is unclear and immediate mitigation decisions are required.
- During Brownouts (degraded SLOs in single region) or Multi-tenant contention events.

Key definitions:
- Priority score: a weighted integer (0-100) combining signals in the table below.
- La...

title: Burst policy, SLA smoothing, and simulated loadbook
preview:
SUMMARY
This playbook describes a unified approach for handling traffic bursts against latency SLOs using three coordinated mechanisms: (1) burst admission policies, (2) SLA smoothing (adaptive target windows and error-budget shaping), and (3) simulated-load scenarios for verification and capacity planning. The goal...
text:
SUMMARY
This playbook describes a unified approach for handling traffic bursts against latency SLOs using three coordinated mechanisms: (1) burst admission policies, (2) SLA smoothing (adaptive target windows and error-budget shaping), and (3) simulated-load scenarios for verification and capacity planning. The goal is to keep P95 and P99 latency SLOs predictable across short-term spikes while limiting cost and preserving error budgets for sustained load.

Scope and audience
- Scope: inference-router, cost-aware-batcher, model-variant-fallback, and the traffic-shaper service.
- Audience: Platform SREs, Serving Runtime, Performance Engineering, and Product engineers owning route-level SLOs.

Motivation
Large variance in upstream request arrival patterns (SDK retries, client-side misconfiguration, bursty user behavior) causes repeated SLO breaches even when average utilization is within...

title: Buildkite GPU provisioning gate and canary strategy
preview:
Summary

This document defines the Buildkite-based gating and canary promotion strategy for GPU provisioning pipelines. It formalizes the safety checks, Terraform module promotion steps, live canary sizing, traffic-shift policy, observable signals used to validate healthy provisioning, and the oncall runbook for rem...
text:
Summary

This document defines the Buildkite-based gating and canary promotion strategy for GPU provisioning pipelines. It formalizes the safety checks, Terraform module promotion steps, live canary sizing, traffic-shift policy, observable signals used to validate healthy provisioning, and the oncall runbook for remediation. The intent is to reduce blast radius from configuration or IaC changes that touch GPU host/instance pools and to provide an automated, repeatable promotion path from feature branches -> staging -> canary -> prod pools.

Goals

- Prevent accidental large-scale GPU pool churn due to IaC or pipeline changes.
- Provide deterministic canary window and success criteria for Terraform changes affecting GPU fleets (node pools, autoscaler configs, taints/labels).
- Standardize Buildkite pipeline stages and gating checks across teams.
- Automate rollback and safe promotion w...

## Decision
For v1 of the Quantization Profile Library, Redwood Inference will expose **quantization profile enablement at the route level** (route config), with optional scoping by region/deployment tier where supported. We will **not** provide a **tenant-wide default e...
text:
## Status
**Accepted (implemented for v1).**

## Decision
For v1 of the Quantization Profile Library, Redwood Inference will expose **quantization profile enablement at the route level** (route config), with optional scoping by region/deployment tier where supported. We will **not** provide a **tenant-wide default enablement** toggle in v1.

In practice:
- Routes can specify `quantization_profile_ref = "default" | "disabled" | "profile:<id>@<version>"` (exact wire shape documented in the control-plane service spec).
- The platform will resolve the route’s effective setting deterministically during policy evaluation and/or routing.
- If a requested profile is incompatible (model/variant/hardware/context constraints) the system will **fallback to baseline precision** and emit a structured signal (metrics + trace/log annotation).
- When the feature is off (no route setting), behavior rem...

title: Authorization Safeguards and Compliance Workflow for Inference Systems

text:
Overview

This runbook defines an integrated, end-to-end workflow for authorization (RBAC/attribute-based checks), audit logging, data residency validation, secure-SDLC gating, incident response interplay, compliance evidence packaging, and risk/exception handling for Redwood Inference model-serving and control-plane systems. It is intended for cross-functional use by Security, SRE, Platform, and Product teams responsible for secure model deployments and tenant isolation.

Goals

- Ensure authorization decisions are evaluated and logged with sufficient forensic detail to support audits and incident investigation.
- Prevent unauthorized cross-residency inference or data access by combining policy checks (RBAC/ABAC), residency metadata, and runtime enforcement.
- Provide a standardized evidence bundle and manifest that maps policy decisions to artifact hashes for regulatory review.

title: Quant profile requirements for Hosted API catalog models
preview:
# Overview
This page defines the minimum set of quantization profiles and validation checks required for a model to be eligible for the Hosted API catalog.

# Why we require multiple profiles
Hosted traffic varies significantly by latency sensitivity and context length. Having pre-validated quantization profiles ens...
text:
# Overview
This page defines the minimum set of quantization profiles and validation checks required for a model to be eligible for the Hosted API catalog.

# Why we require multiple profiles
Hosted traffic varies significantly by latency sensitivity and context length. Having pre-validated quantization profiles ensures we can route cost-sensitive workloads without surprise quality regressions.

# Required profiles (per model)
For every new catalog model, you must produce and validate *at least* the following profiles:
- `fp16` (reference)
- `int8-weight-only` (cost-optimized)
- `int4-weight-only` (when supported by kernels/hardware; if not supported, document as N/A with Runtime owner signoff)

# Validation checks (required)
Each profile must pass:
1. **Correctness smoke** (Owner: Serving Runtime)
 - Generate determinism check (same seed, same output distribution tolerance)
 - Tokeni...

title: Offline artifact bundles for Redwood Private (disconnected installs)
preview:

h1. When to use an offline bundle

text:
h1. Purpose
This page defines the standard approach for delivering *offline artifact bundles* for Redwood Private deployments where outbound internet access is not available (or is prohibited by policy).

h1. When to use an offline bundle
Use an offline bundle when a customer environment has one or more of the following:
* No outbound internet (no NAT / no proxy egress).
* No ability to pull from external registries (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR public).
* Restricted certificate validation (no public OCSP/CRL).

h1. Bundle contents (minimum)
* Helm chart package (versioned).
* Container images as tar archives (or pre-pushed to a customer registry).
* File containing *image digests* (pinned) and recommended tags.
* Checksums for all bundle artifacts.
* "What needs mirroring" checklist (registry, charts, optional model weights if applicable).

h1. Installation approach (high level)
# Customer ...

title: Terraform validation harness for GPU topology changes with simulated traffic
preview:

The goal is to ...
text:
Summary

This playbook documents the recommended Terraform validation harness used to safely change GPU pool topology (instance types, AZ placement, placement groups, taints/tolerations) by running an automated, CI-driven simulation of representative inference traffic against a temporary environment. The goal is to detect capacity, latency, and egress regressions before Terraform changes reach production gates.

Scope

- Applies to Terraform changes that modify: GPU pool sizes, instance flavors, network egress routes, subnets, placement groups, or tenant QoS tags.
- Intended for use in the main infrastructure CI pipeline (Buildkite) and also as a manual preflight for large migration windows.

Why we run simulated traffic during TF validation

Terraform plan/validate and policy-as-code checks catch many problems (iam, security, drift). However, changes that affect topology can cause em...

Overview

Purpose: This playbook describes how Redwood orchestrates compliance evidence collection across RBAC/auth systems, audit logging, data residency boundaries, and retention policies. It defines owners, automation patterns, runbooks for pre-audit packaging and incident-led evidence collection, and the exception/risk workflow. It is intended for security engineers, platform owners, and compliance reviewers.

Scope
- Systems in-scope: auth-service (OIDC), rbac-manager (policy engine), audit-ingest (Kafka -> S3), audit-search (Elasticsearch), kms-yggdrasil (KMS/HSM integration), evidence-orchestrator (serverless job runner), compliance-indexer.
- Regions: us-east-1, eu-west-1, ap-southeast-1.
- Evidence types: audit records, configuration snapshots, access certification logs, key rotation proofs, data residency attestations, code-signing manifests, vulnerability scan reports.

title: Failure-domain-aware capacity scheduling and SLO-safe deployments
preview:
Overview:
This playbook describes a failure-domain-aware approach to capacity scheduling and deployment gating designed to protect SLOs and preserve error budget during planned and unplanned failures. It combines quantitative headroom provisioning, progressive rollouts, and automated rollback/eviction rules that are...
text:
Overview:
This playbook describes a failure-domain-aware approach to capacity scheduling and deployment gating designed to protect SLOs and preserve error budget during planned and unplanned failures. It combines quantitative headroom provisioning, progressive rollouts, and automated rollback/eviction rules that are failure-domain–sensitive (region / AZ / rack / GPU-pool / node). The goal is to keep user-visible SLOs stable while making predictable capacity changes for model serving and multi-tenant workloads.

Scope:
- Applies to all production inference pools managed by Redwood (hosted, dedicated, private control plane).
- Primary targets: text generation, chat, and embedding routes where p95 latency and availability SLOs are observable.
- Excludes: offline batch/ETL jobs (handled by batch capacity playbook).

Key goals:
- Ensure SLO preservation during single and multi-domain degra...

title: SLO-aware dependency graph and release guardrails
preview:
Summary
-------
This decision documents a standardized approach for (1) annotating the runtime dependency graph with SLO risk metadata, and (2) enforcing release guardrails that adapt canary sizing, traffic routing, and automated rollback thresholds based on SLO risk tiers. The goal is to reduce blast radius from de...
text:
Summary
-------
This decision documents a standardized approach for (1) annotating the runtime dependency graph with SLO risk metadata, and (2) enforcing release guardrails that adapt canary sizing, traffic routing, and automated rollback thresholds based on SLO risk tiers. The goal is to reduce blast radius from deployments and align rollout policy with customer-facing reliability targets.

Motivation
----------
We operate a set of loosely coupled inference and control-plane services with varying criticality to customer latency and correctness (e.g., routing, model-serving, kv-cache, billing). Historically releases have used a fixed canary ratio and static health checks; this mismatches business risk for high-SLO paths and increases manual review overhead. By embedding SLO metadata into the dependency graph and driving release policies from that metadata, we can: 
- Prioritize safety...

The Sandbox is a self-service environment for short-term experiments, model profiling, microbenchmarks, and pre-production evals. It balances developer velocity...
text:
Overview

Purpose: This page defines governance, quota rules, and the operator playbook for Redwood Inference's GPU Experimentation Sandbox (the ‘Sandbox’). The Sandbox is a self-service environment for short-term experiments, model profiling, microbenchmarks, and pre-production evals. It balances developer velocity with cost control, safety, and fair-share across teams.

Scope
- Environment: ephemeral Sandbox clusters (non-production, tagged `sandbox/*`).
- Workloads: jobs that are exploratory, short-duration (<= 48h default) or flagged as non-customer-facing.
- Not allowed: production inference endpoints, regulated data processing, and customer PII workloads. See Security policy for exceptions.

Goals
1. Provide low-latency self-service GPU access for developers and researchers.
2. Ensure predictable cost and prevent noisy-neighbor impact on production pools.
3. Enforce per-team quo...

title: GPU offline capacity reclamation and tenant thaw protocol
preview:
Summary
This runbook describes the end-to-end process for reclaiming GPU capacity that has been declared offline (hardware fault, maintenance, prolonged preemption or lost heartbeats), and the controlled "thaw" procedure for returning tenants/workloads to that reclaimed capacity. The goal is to minimize customer imp...
text:
Summary
This runbook describes the end-to-end process for reclaiming GPU capacity that has been declared offline (hardware fault, maintenance, prolonged preemption or lost heartbeats), and the controlled "thaw" procedure for returning tenants/workloads to that reclaimed capacity. The goal is to minimize customer impact, preserve SLOs, keep billing/credits consistent, and ensure Terraform/IaC and CI/CD artifacts remain the source of truth.

When to use this doc
- A node or GPU is marked OFFLINE by fleet telemetry for > 5 minutes and is not automatically rejoining.
- Eviction/maintenance window has completed but the node failed health checks.
- A region-level cold-power recovery or rack maintenance requires manual reclamation.

Owner and escalation
- Primary owner: GPU Fleet Capacity team (oncall: gpu-capacity@pagerduty)
- Secondary owner: Cloud Accounts & Networking (for VPC/egress iss...

title: SLI contract friction and cross-team learning sprint retrospective (Nov 2024)
preview:
Overview:

This retrospective captures the outcomes from the two-week cross-team learning sprint (Nov 4–18, 2024) that emerged after multiple small but persistent SLI contract breaks across platform components. The goal of the sprint was to: (1) surface where SLI definitions and alert contracts diverged between team...
text:
Overview:

This retrospective captures the outcomes from the two-week cross-team learning sprint (Nov 4–18, 2024) that emerged after multiple small but persistent SLI contract breaks across platform components. The goal of the sprint was to: (1) surface where SLI definitions and alert contracts diverged between teams, (2) produce concrete dashboard and alert changes that reduce noisy page wakes, and (3) update operator-facing runbooks to speed diagnosis.

Summary of findings:
- Multiple teams (allocator, prefetch-controller, router, kv-prefix-cache) had subtly different interpretations of the same SLIs (e.g., \"token_cost_per_request\" vs \"token_cost_per_user_request\").
- Alerts were firing during legitimate transient load patterns due to brittle burn-rate thresholds and missing context panels (tenant, region, model_variant).
- Operators lacked a compact decision canvas that maps an...

These gates apply whether the rollout is per...
text:
# Overview
This page defines the **evaluation readiness gates** required before shipping a new **Hosted API** model version (or changing serving configuration that can impact outputs). The goal is to prevent quality regressions while keeping rollout velocity predictable.

These gates apply whether the rollout is performed as a normal canary or as part of a fallback-enabled deployment.

# Scope
**In scope:** chat/completions models exposed via the Hosted API, model version bumps, tokenizer changes, system prompt changes, safety policy changes, quantization/profile changes that materially alter outputs.

**Out of scope:** embeddings-only model refreshes (see Embeddings Rollout Gates), internal-only experimental endpoints.

# Required artifacts (must be attached in the release ticket)
1. **Model build identifier** (registry tag) and release candidate name.
2. **Prompt set selection**: wh...

text:
Summary
-------
This document defines a standard, audit-friendly framework for annotating GPU-based inference telemetry and deriving tenant-level cost attribution. The goal is to make per-request and per-batch cost signals reliable, low-overhead, and actionable across hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

Intended audience
-----------------
- Infra engineers responsible for GPU fleet, billing pipeline, and Prometheus/Otel instrumentation.
- Platform engineers who implement request routing, batching, and tenant isolation.
- SREs and finance partners who consume cost reports and SLO-aligned billing.

Context and motivation
----------------------
We run mixed workloads (hosted API + dedicated + private). Current gaps we repeatedly see: inconsistent tenant tagging across layers, missing kernel-level telemetry for quantized workloads, and cost reports that lag or misattribute warm-p...

title: GPU Kernel Rollout and Telemetry Gating — Runbook
preview:
Summary

This runbook describes the safe rollout pattern for kernel-level changes (CUDA kernels, custom fused kernels, or runtime micro-optimizations) that affect the GPU serving runtime. The goal is to deploy new kernels with a telemetry gating strategy that minimizes user impact while enabling quick rollback if re...
text:
Summary

This runbook describes the safe rollout pattern for kernel-level changes (CUDA kernels, custom fused kernels, or runtime micro-optimizations) that affect the GPU serving runtime. The goal is to deploy new kernels with a telemetry gating strategy that minimizes user impact while enabling quick rollback if regressions are detected.

When to use this runbook

- Any change that modifies low-level GPU kernels, JIT codepaths, or scheduling hooks that could influence latency, error surface, or memory usage.
- Binary changes to the runtime Docker image that include optimized kernels.
- Enabling/disabling a kernel feature flag that affects model inference execution.

Prerequisites

- Owner: runtime-team (oncall rotation listed in Team Roster page).
- CI: Buildkite job "gpu-kernel-bake" configured and green.
- Canary pool: "gpu-canary-pool" (tagged fleet with 2 x A100-80GB and 4 x A10s...
